quotes.speaker: Harold Danko
quotes.text: Yeah sometimes, and some tunes just to remind myself too, to get back to see where I am. And I think keeping the melody close is very important. And also it’s a reference point and if you can develop that, you know Lee Konitz had this kind of one to ten thing where if you’re playing the melody that’s number one and if you’re playing something that’s totally unlike the melody, you know if you’re playing bebop maybe that’s number five or number six. If you’re playing really abstractly that’s number ten, but you’re still playing the tune. So Lee was very specific about saying yeah you should be able to play jazz at number two and number three, that that still sounds like jazz. Because a lot of people just have their prepared lick repertoire or whatever, and so they play the melody and then they go into whatever. And he said they’re jumping maybe from number one to number six, and it’s like so how do you — and so there’s a thing that I’m on with Lee it’s called “Portrait of a Saxophonist as an Artist” it’s a video that we did in Canada. And Lee is trying to get this flute player to play something close to the melody. Because if you play these other things, and when he played close to the melody it didn’t sound like jazz you know, it just sounded suddenly stiff. And so that’s the point of that, and then funny in that video the next thing goes to Lee playing very abstractly on “Stella by Starlight” or something like that, just the most — but you know Lee had me singing Lester Young solos and Louis Armstrong. I basically did not study with Lee but in the course of rehearsing I got a lot of the things that he taught people. And he was very insistent on, like Lee once said, once told me I don’t know I was playing “All the Things You Are” or something, and he accused me of using too much technique. I said man you want chops, there’s a hundred guys in New York — I didn’t say this but I’m thinking like you want chops? I’m not one of the chop guys. There’s guys that will play rings around me. You want chops? So Lee said, “Well it should basically be able to play a good solo with one finger.” And he’d be like [plays piano] whatever it was you know. So okay yeah. So one of the things I have to remind myself of, it came really in handy because the first time I played Carnegie Hall with Chet Baker, the first time in Carnegie Hall, I remember we were playing “Tidal Breeze” my tune. And I looked down and my knee was going up and down, I had this nervous thing. And I thought of that thing that Lee — I said I know my tune I can just kind of play the melody, I can play really close and just let the rhythm section groove. And then my knee calmed down and I was able to — but it was like one of those little nervous things, like the tic that could define your career. So that thing of being able to play it and have it sound like it’s jazz but very simple. It’s really hard, and it’s like I want to show what I can do sometimes, even at my age. But sometimes it’s just © Fillius Jazz Archive -14-
quotes.caption: First Carnegie Hall performance with Chet Baker
quotes.doc_title: Harold Danko interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, April 28, 2021
